nativeRange.habitat: Wild Bucephalandra from the Kedagang region of Borneo — in the area spanning Indonesian Kalimantan and Sarawak — grow as rheophytes (plants adapted to life in fast-moving water) on rocks and submerged wood along swift rainforest streams, where fluctuating water levels periodically leave them exposed above the surface before resubmerging them.

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line
do not bury the rhizome in substrate — leave it exposed to the water column
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; commonly used in foreground or midground placements
stable, established tank — generally advised against introducing into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra 'Kedagang' is a named cultivar from Borneo, among the larger and faster-growing varieties in the genus. Its dark green oblong leaves carry small white dots and a blue metallic iridescence when submerged, with dark red rhizomes and copper-bronze leaf undersides.
careNotes: Bucephalandra 'Kedagang' is a named cultivar of the genus Bucephalandra (family Araceae), endemic to the island of Borneo. Like all Bucephalandra, it is an epiphyte — a plant that anchors to hard surfaces rather than rooting into substrate — so the creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves and roots grow) should be attached to lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line, and left exposed to the water column. Burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause it to rot. A distinctive feature noted by hobbyists is the copper-bronze coloration on the leaf undersides, which contrasts with the dark green upper surface and the blue structural iridescence visible when submerged. Lighting is flexible: low light supports steady growth, while medium light tends to bring out the structural-color iridescence more fully. CO2 injection is generally considered optional but tends to produce noticeably better growth and color. The plant absorbs nutrients from the water column, so substrate type is largely irrelevant and root tabs are not generally needed; light liquid fertilization is sufficient. 'Buce melt' — the loss of the original emersed-grown leaves after first submersion — is widely reported across the genus and should be expected with newly acquired tissue-cultured or potted Kedagang. As long as the rhizome remains firm, new submersed-form leaves are generally reported to emerge over the following weeks. Propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean scissors, ensuring each division has leaves, roots, and at least a few centimetres of rhizome, then attach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape.
breedingNotes: Hobby propagation is by rhizome division. Once the plant is established, cut the rhizome at a natural bend or constriction with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, then reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction — flowering via the genus' characteristic spathe and spadix (a hooded leaf-like bract enclosing a central spike that bears the flowers) — is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical propagation method for most hobbyists.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Aquarium Co-Op describes the Kedagang cultivar as dark green oblong leaves with wavy edges ~6 cm long by 1-2 cm wide, small white dots, iridescent blue sheen, dark red rhizomes, mature height ~10-20 cm, and one of the faster-growing buce cultivars. Offered as tissue culture.

additional.name: KeepFishKeeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked Borneo origin, dark green narrow leaves with white dots up to ~6 cm long, red rhizomes, and blue metallic submerged coloration. Page does not give explicit water parameters; husbandry parameters inherited from the parent genus entry (fw-plant-016).

additional.name: AquaMate parent entry: Bucephalandra (fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Genus-level husbandry (water parameters 22-28 °C / pH 6-7.5 / gH 3-15, low-to-medium light, optional CO2, rhizome-attachment to hardscape, propagation by rhizome division, 'buce melt' acclimation) used as the canonical care reference for this cultivar; care for named cultivars including 'Kedagang' is reported across hobby sources to be essentially identical to the parent genus.
